title: Detailed Geometry and Appearance from Opportunistic Motion

headings.text: Motion-Aware Joint Optimization via Soft-Masked Transform
Motion-Aware Appearance Modeling via Radiance Probing
Meshing with Viewpoints from All Frames
Additional Qualitative Results on Synthetic Data
Additional Qualitative Results on Real-World Datasets

section_anchors.text: We show that this bound can be broken by exploiting opportunistic object motion
We address these by formulating a joint pose and shape optimization using 2D Gaussian splatting
Recovering 3D geometry from sparse, static views is inherently ill-posed.
In this paper, we exploit the movement of an object to recover higher-resolution geometry and appearance from just a handful of static cameras.
Our approach derives a compact yet expressive appearance model that considers pose-dependent changes in both diffuse and specular reflections.
Since our method is based on 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS), we briefly review it here
Given sparse-view videos captured by V static cameras (e.g., V = 4 corner cameras), our goal is to reconstruct the geometry and appearance of a rigidly moving object with high-fidelity.
We bootstrap our alternating estimation by initializing the canonical Gaussians from the first frames of the V views using MAtCha Gaussians [13]
Using multi-view observations from subsequent timesteps, we alternate between estimating per-frame object poses and refining the canonical Gaussian parameters.
section_anchors.expected_section: Motion-Aware Joint Optimization via Soft-Masked Transform

section_anchors.text: Following the alternating optimization phase, we perform a joint refinement of geometry and appearance using the full temporal sequence.
section_anchors.expected_section: Motion-Aware Appearance Modeling via Radiance Probing

section_anchors.text: Once the joint optimization is complete, we extract a surface mesh of the foreground object.
section_anchors.expected_section: Meshing with Viewpoints from All Frames

section_anchors.text: We validate the effectiveness of our method using a newly created synthetic multi-view video dataset and established real-world datasets.
We first quantitatively evaluate our method using synthetic data with ground-truth surface details.
We further evaluate our method on several real-world datasets.
We introduced a method to recover the 3D shape, appearance, and time-varying poses of a rigidly moving object from static, sparse-view RGB videos.
In Fig. 8 and the supplementary video, we test our method on in-the-wild videos captured by ourselves.
The learnable parameters of our method are the parameters of 2D Gaussians, the appearance parameters shared across the object (θs and θd ), and per-frame object poses
The synthetic dataset consists of multi-view videos of five object-environment pairs
For the HO3D dataset (a hand-object interaction dataset), we use the sequences that provide multi-view images
For the HODome dataset (high-fidelity capture of human-object interaction), we use the box, chair, desk, table, talltable, trashcan, and trolleycase sequences
Figure 9 shows additional qualitative results of the surface normals recovered from the synthetic dataset.
section_anchors.expected_section: Additional Qualitative Results on Synthetic Data

section_anchors.text: Figures 12 and 13 show qualitative results of the ablation studies on the diffuse and specular components of our motion-aware appearance model.
To further evaluate the effectiveness of our alternating estimation framework, we test our method under more challenging temporally sparse settings.
We also evaluate the effectiveness of refining the per-Gaussian segmentation mask values mi .
Figure 18 shows additional qualitative results of the reconstructed surface geometry on real-world datasets.
section_anchors.expected_section: Additional Qualitative Results on Real-World Datasets
